---
title: "Difference-in-Differences"
---

# Scope

This note will explain how difference-in-differences identifies causal effects by comparing changes over time in treated and untreated groups [@cunningham2018differences; @gertler2010ch7difference].

# Planned Sections

## What The Method Is

- Two-period intuition
- Group-by-time comparison
- ATT interpretation in the canonical setup

## When To Use It

- Policy or program adoption observed over time
- Treated and untreated units both observed before and after treatment
- No credible cutoff design and no clearly random assignment

## Core Assumptions

- Parallel trends
- No anticipatory behavior, or explicit treatment of anticipation
- Stable group composition, or explicit handling of changes

## Data Requirements

- Panel or repeated cross-section data
- Clear treatment timing
- Meaningful pre-treatment outcome observations

## Workflow

1. Define treatment timing and comparison groups.
2. Plot outcomes over time before estimation.
3. Inspect whether pre-treatment trends are plausibly parallel.
4. Estimate the baseline DiD model.
5. Check robustness with event-study or alternative specifications.

## Diagnostics And Risks

- Pre-trend checks
- Differential shocks
- Composition changes
- Staggered adoption complications

## Worked Example

A rollout of a local employment support policy across areas, with outcomes observed before and after implementation.

## Software

- `fixest`
- `did`
- `ggplot2`
